headline: Oracle Q4 FY26 blowout — RPO $638B (+363% YoY, vs $589.5B Street); OCI +93% to $5.8B; stock -10% AH on $70B FY27 capex shock
summary: Oracle delivered a top-to-bottom Q4 beat: revenue $19.2B (+21% YoY, beat $19.1B Street), total cloud $9.9B (+47%), OCI $5.8B (+93% vs $5.17B consensus), GAAP EPS $1.45 (+21%) and non-GAAP $2.11 (+24% vs $1.97 Street). RPO landed at a record $638B, +363% YoY and +$85B sequential — comfortably above the $589.5B Street bar. FY26 revenue closed at $67.4B (+17%) and non-GAAP EPS at $7.63 (+27%). But the print was sold hard: FY26 capex came in at $55.7B (vs $50B guide), and FY27 capex was guided to $70B PLUS a separate $20-25B prepayment for components — implying total ~$90-95B fiscal outlay against a $90B revenue target. The company also flagged a ~$20B incremental capital raise. ORCL fell 10-11% premarket below $180, despite FY27 cloud guide of +58-64% USD. Read-through is structurally bullish for the AI compute stack — NVDA, AMD, AVGO, MU, Hynix/Samsung HBM, DELL, SMCI, VRT — and validates the AI-infrastructure demand thesis at scale. Negative on ORCL margins and free cash flow trajectory near term.

headline: AI hardware bid premarket on Oracle $70B capex guide — NVDA, AMD, DELL, SMCI, MU all up; Oracle becomes anchor TAM signal
summary: With Oracle disclosing $70B in FY27 net capex plus $20-25B in component prepayments, premarket flows rotated into the suppliers feeding hyperscale buildouts. NVDA +1-2%, AMD higher, DELL, SMCI rebounding from yesterday's dilution slide, and MU +3% on $1.3B premarket volume despite Tuesday's profit-taking. The Oracle disclosure is the largest single-buyer capex commitment outside of MSFT/META/GOOGL — and the $75B cumulative AI-contract investment customers have made to Oracle implies the demand is contractually anchored, not speculative. Names with the highest read-through exposure: NVDA (GB300/Rubin GPUs), MU/Hynix/Samsung (HBM4), VRT and CLS (rack integration), CRWV (overlapping customer base / GPU cloud comp), AVGO (ASIC + networking). The Wedbush 'pick-and-shovel' frame on the AI build is reinforced.

headline: Apple WWDC 2026: Apple Intelligence + Gemini-powered Siri unveiled; Tim Cook's final keynote; AAPL -3%
summary: At WWDC 2026, Apple introduced 'Apple Intelligence' as its next-generation AI platform, with a profoundly more capable Siri that leverages a hybrid model architecture — Apple's own models running alongside Google's Gemini for multimodal/context-aware features. New MacOS named 'Golden Gate.' Tim Cook confirmed this was his final WWDC keynote as CEO, marking a CEO succession watch as an open agenda item. AAPL fell more than 3% post-announcement, giving back recent all-time-high gains, as investors questioned (1) Apple's reliance on Gemini for headline AI features (implied moat erosion vs OpenAI, Meta, Google); (2) whether the Siri redesign is enough to monetize. Read-through is positive for GOOGL (already running ~$1B/yr Apple Intelligence distribution deal on Siri search per last week's reporting), and incrementally neutral-to-negative for the proprietary frontier LLM thesis (OpenAI, Anthropic).

headline: SK Hynix equipment suppliers request 3-4% price hikes; memory supercycle pricing power spreads upstream
summary: In the Asian session, multiple Tier-1 semiconductor equipment suppliers formally requested 3-4% supply price increases from SK Hynix, citing capacity-build constraints and delivery-schedule leverage. Hynix has asked vendors to submit supporting documentation pending evaluation. With Hynix 2026 capex now expected to far exceed the 2025 ~$20B baseline (SK Chairman Chey Tae-won committed to double wafer capacity within 5 years and triple by 2034), equipment suppliers' bargaining power is structurally improving. Direct beneficiaries: AMAT (recently +$590 Barclays PT), LRCX, KLAC, Tokyo Electron (8035.T closed +2.54%), ASML, Entegris (ENTG), Shin-Etsu (4063.T). This is the second-order signal of the memory supercycle leaking into the wafer-fab equipment (WFE) layer.

headline: Asia V-shaped reversal: KOSPI +0.42% to 7,763.95, Nikkei +0.06% to 64,217.22; SK Hynix +2.59%, Kioxia +7%, Tokyo Electron +2.54%
summary: Asia closed off morning lows after a V-shaped reversal. KOSPI ended +0.42% at 7,763.95 (after opening sharply lower on Iran/Hormuz + US CPI); Nikkei +0.06% at 64,217.22. SK Hynix closed +2.59% at KRW 2,101,000 — reversing intraday weakness after SK Chairman Chey Tae-won outlined the wafer-capacity tripling plan. Kioxia (285A) ripped +7% on tight NAND supply + ongoing analyst rerating. Tokyo Electron +2.54% on WFE pricing power. Samsung Electronics -1.16% to KRW 299,000 — gave back early gains tied to news that the OpenAI CEO is visiting Korea next week. SoftBank Group -1.35%. The memory complex is digesting the macro overhang (Hormuz, BOJ uncertainty) but the structural supercycle narrative continues to bid the trade.

headline: BOJ Governor Ueda hospitalized; deputy Himino chairs June 15-16 meeting; July hike to 1% still seen on track
summary: The Bank of Japan announced Wednesday evening that Governor Kazuo Ueda (74) has been hospitalized for treatment of an infected liver cyst and will miss the June 15-16 policy meeting. Deputy Governor Ryozo Himino will preside; Deputy Governor Shinichi Uchida will host the post-meeting press conference. Ueda is expected to attend the July 30-31 meeting. Market consensus still calls for the BOJ to lift the policy rate to 1.00% from 0.75% at the July meeting — a 3-decade high — but the headline added near-term JPY/JGB volatility. Modest tail risk for Japan semi-equipment names (8035.T, 6920.JP, 4063.T) given the FX/duration sensitivity, though the AI memory bid offset most of the noise in today's session.

headline: Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed; tankers anchored; Iran/US escalation persists into PPI print
summary: The Strait of Hormuz remains largely blocked to commercial transit, with Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM and Hapag-Lloyd continuing to avoid the chokepoint. Over 150 tankers are reportedly anchored outside the strait awaiting the all-clear. ~20% of global oil and ~20% of global LNG flow through the corridor; China sources ~40% of its crude imports here, Japan ~70% of its Middle East crude. Following Tuesday's brief de-escalation chatter, Wednesday's reversal (Trump's 'pay the price' line + IRGC mining/boarding actions) has put the risk premium back on. Risk-off pressure on duration AI longs at the open; bid for 'fortress energy' (GEV, ETN, VRT, CEG, VST, NEE) and oil-linked names. WTI testing the $90s. The premium persists into the June 17 FOMC.

headline: May PPI + jobless claims at 8:30 AM ET: PPI expected +0.7% MoM / +6.4% YoY (vs +6.0% prior); claims 220K (vs 225K)
summary: Today's pre-bell macro double-header. PPI consensus is +0.7% MoM (vs +1.4% prior) and +6.4% YoY (vs +6.0%) — note the YoY acceleration baked in, which would be the third consecutive monthly step-up. Initial jobless claims consensus 220K (vs 225K prior); continuing claims 1,780K (vs 1,777K). With core CPI yesterday printing a benign +0.2% MoM, today's PPI is the last macro datapoint before the June 17 FOMC blackout. A hot PPI would re-pressure the duration-sensitive AI cohort already digesting the Oracle capex shock; an in-line print keeps the Fed-hold narrative intact. Watch wholesale services categories most closely — they feed the core PCE component the Fed actually targets.

headline: Adobe Q2 FY26 AMC tonight; ADBE down ~30% YTD as Street tests the 'AI eats Photoshop' thesis
summary: Adobe reports fiscal Q2 2026 after the close Thursday — the cleanest live test yet of whether generative AI cannibalizes or expands Creative Cloud. Consensus: $5.81 non-GAAP EPS / $6.45B revenue, implying ~15% EPS growth and ~10% revenue growth YoY. Adobe's own guide: $6.43-6.48B revenue, $5.85-5.90 EPS. Firefly ending ARR crossed $250M in Q1 (AI-first ARR more than doubled YoY). With ADBE down ~30% YTD on disruption fears (Sora video, Midjourney, etc.), the buy-side focus is (1) AI-ARR acceleration trajectory and (2) management's explicit framing of the AI monetization model (per-seat vs consumption vs new SKUs). Read-through to other 'AI-disrupted' software incumbents (NOW, CRM, INTU) — and to the LLM platform layer (MSFT/Copilot, GOOGL/Gemini in Apple) — depending on print/guide.
